title: Black Tea on Cotton
stainChemistry: Black tea is a pure tannin stain. On cotton it bonds to the cellulose fibers as a pale-to-medium brown ring. Left alone, tannin oxidizes and darkens into a set stain that is much harder to lift.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flushes the soluble tannin out of the cotton before it oxidizes and sets. Gentle blotting (never rubbing) lifts the residue without driving it deeper into the weave. Mild detergent followed by a mild acid-side tannin treatment releases the remaining tannin trace, and oxygen bleach handles any shadow without the alkaline-side chemistry that would darken a pure tannin stain.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot the spill immediately with a clean white cloth, then flush cold water through the back of the stain. Keep blotting — never rub — until the transfer slows.

spottingProtocol.technique: Work gently with fingertips or a soft cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of mild liquid detergent or dish soap to the damp stain. Work gently in circles, then rinse with cold water. Blot dry between rinses.
If a pale ring remains, apply a mild acid-side tannin treatment, blot gently, then rinse again. This helps release the last of the tannin trace.
If a shadow still shows, use a color-safe oxygen bleach solution and monitor the area closely, then rinse thoroughly. Do not use chlorine bleach on dyed cotton.
Wash the item per the care label and air dry until you confirm the stain is gone. Do not apply heat (dryer or iron) until the area is completely clear — heat permanently sets any tannin residue.

homeSolutions.instruction: Blot immediately with a clean white cloth, then flush cold water through the back of the stain. Never rub tannin stains.
Work in a few drops of mild dish soap or liquid detergent and rinse well with cold water.
If a faint ring remains, apply a mild acid-side tannin treatment and rinse again.
If needed, use a color-safe oxygen bleach soak before washing. Do not use chlorine bleach on dyed cotton.

materialWarnings: Never rub — blot only. Rubbing drives tannin deeper into the weave.
Do not use hot or warm water until the stain is fully gone — heat sets tannin permanently.
Never use ammonia or alkaline bleach (including chlorine) on pure tannin — alkali darkens tannin into a set stain.
Test oxygen bleach first if the cotton is dyed or trimmed.

escalation.when: Escalate if the stain is old, heat-set, on a dry-clean-only cotton garment, or if repeated home treatment leaves a visible ring.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner it is black tea on cotton and whether detergent, acid-side tannin treatment, or oxygen bleach were already used. Note how long before it was treated.

consumer.name: Mild Liquid Dish Soap
consumer.note: Use only a small amount and rinse fully with cold water.

consumer.name: Tannin Spotter
consumer.note: Use a mild acid-side tannin treatment labeled safe for fabric spot work.

consumer.name: Oxygen Bleach
consumer.note: Use a color-safe oxygen bleach. Do not use chlorine bleach on dyed cotton.

professional.name: Tannin Spotter (acid-side)
professional.use: Stubborn residual ring after home treatment
professional.note: Acid-side only on pure tannin. Never combine with ammonia or alkaline agents.

customerExplanation: Black tea on cotton is a standard tannin cleanup — if you catch it fresh and blot with cold water, most of the spill comes out before it sets. The key rules: cold water, blot not rub, no heat until the stain is confirmed gone, and avoid ammonia-side chemistry on pure tannin.
commonMistakes: Rubbing or scrubbing instead of blotting — drives tannin into the weave
Using hot or warm water — sets the tannin permanently
Putting the garment in the dryer before confirming the stain is gone
Do not use ammonia or chlorine bleach — alkali darkens tannin into a set stain
scienceNote: Tea tannins (tannic acid and related polyphenols) bond readily to cotton cellulose through hydrogen bonding. They stay water-soluble when fresh, which is why cold-water flushing works best before oxidation sets in. Heat accelerates oxidation and covalent cross-linking with the cellulose, turning a removable spill into a permanent stain. Pure tannin protocols avoid ammonia and chlorine bleach entirely — alkali substances deprotonate the polyphenol structure and darken the stain, which is why pure-tannin chemistry stays acid-side only.
